<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The logic looks correct. When we're on the list with URL ?page=1&search=5967698:
1. returnQueryRef.current = searchParams.toString() = "page=1&search=5967698"
2. sessionStorage is set in the effect when searchParams change
3. When they click View we do: const q = returnQueryRef.current || searchParams.toString(); sessionStorage.setItem('invoicesReturnQuery', q); navigate(..., { state: { returnQuery: q } });

So q should be "page=1&search=5967698". On the view page we read fromState = location.state?.returnQuery. In production, maybe location.state is undefined? So we'd use sessionStorage. We should have set it. Unless - the issue is that we're not including searchParams in the sync effect's dependency array, so we're comparing nextStr !== searchParams.toString() but searchParams is a stable reference that might not reflect the latest URL? No, searchParams from useSearchParams() updates when the URL changes.

Wait - one possibility: **maybe when we call setSearchParams(next, { replace: true }), the searchParams don't update until the next render**. So we have: user clicks Search, setActiveSearchQuery('5967698'). Re-render. Sync effect runs. We build next = "page=1&search=5967698". We compare nextStr !== searchParams.toString(). At this moment searchParams is from the current render - it might still be "page=1". So we call setSearchParams(next). So we're good. So in the next render, searchParams would be "page=1&search=5967698". So returnQueryRef.current would be updated to that in the render. So when the user clicks View (could be immediately or a second later), we're in a render where searchParams is "page=1&search=5967698". So we're good.

Unless - **the sync effect doesn't run because of the dependency array**. We have [pagination.page, activeSearchQuery, ...]. We don't have searchParams. So when the user sets activeSearchQuery, the sync effect runs. We build next. We compare to searchParams.toString(). So we should be good.

Let me check if there's something specific about how React Router or the production build might strip state. One common issue: **when using createBrowserRouter or similar, the state might not be passed if the navigation is done in a way that causes a full remount**. Or if the app is deployed in a way that the view page is a separate chunk and the state is lost.

A more reliable approach: **always pass the return URL in the view page's URL as a query param** when navigating from list to view. So instead of (or in addition to) state and sessionStorage, we could add ?returnTo= or encode the return query in the URL. Then the view page would read it from searchParams. That way we're not relying on state or sessionStorage at all for the "from list" case. For example: navigate(`/invoices/${id}/view?returnQuery=${encodeURIComponent(searchParams.toString())}`). Then on the view page we'd read: const returnQuery = location.state?.returnQuery || new URLSearchParams(location.search).get('returnQuery') || sessionStorage.getItem(...) || `page=${listPage}`. So we'd have three sources: state, URL param, sessionStorage. The URL param would persist across reloads and wouldn't be lost. Let me implement that - pass returnQuery as a URL param when navigating from list to view. That way even if state is stripped and sessionStorage is cleared, we'd have it in the URL.

Actually wait - if we put it in the URL, then when the user bookmarks the view page or shares it, the URL would have returnQuery in it which might be weird. So we could use a short-lived approach: only add returnQuery to the URL when navigating from the list. When the view page loads, we read it from the URL and then optionally remove it (replaceState) so the URL is clean. So: navigate(`/invoices/${id}/view?returnQuery=${encodeURIComponent(q)}`, { state: { returnQuery: q } }). On view page: const fromUrl = new URLSearchParams(location.search).get('returnQuery'); const returnQuery = location.state?.returnQuery || fromUrl || sessionStorage.getItem('invoicesReturnQuery') || ...; Then we could do useEffect(() => { if (fromUrl) { window.history.replaceState(..., remove returnQuery from URL); } }, []); to clean the URL. That way we have maximum reliability.

Let me try the URL param approach - pass returnQuery as a query param when navigating to view/edit, and on the view page read from state first, then from URL search param, then sessionStorage, then default. That should fix the issue regardless of why state/sessionStorage might not be working.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I see a potential issue: **returnQueryRef.current** is set during render: `returnQueryRef.current = searchParams.toString();`. So it's set to whatever searchParams was in the *last* render. When the user first lands on the list with ?page=1, we have searchParams = "page=1". They type search and click Search. activeSearchQuery becomes "5967698". Sync effect runs and calls setSearchParams(next) with "page=1&search=5967698". So we get a re-render. In that re-render, searchParams should now be the new value. So returnQueryRef.current = searchParams.toString() should be "page=1&search=5967698".

Unless - **maybe the sync effect doesn't run in the same tick as the Search click?** Or there's batching. So: user clicks Search. setActiveSearchQuery('5967698') is called. React schedules re-render. Before the re-render, could the user already have clicked View? Unlikely - they'd have to click View in the same moment. So after the re-render, we have the new state. The sync effect runs (because activeSearchQuery is in the dependency array). So we call setSearchParams. That might trigger another re-render (navigation/location update). So we might have two re-renders. So after both, returnQueryRef.current should be "page=1&search=5967698".

One more possibility: **Maybe in production, the list page is not the same "session" - e.g. they're opening the view in a new tab?** So they right-click "Open in new tab" on View. Then the new tab doesn't have location.state (because it's a new tab), and sessionStorage might be shared but we might not have written to it yet in the new tab's context? No, sessionStorage is per-origin and shared across tabs. So when they clicked View in the first tab we set sessionStorage. If they opened in new tab, the view would load in the new tab and read sessionStorage - we'd have set it in the first tab when they clicked. So we're good.

Let me try adding **returnQuery as a URL search param** when navigating to view/edit. So we'd do:
navigate(`/invoices/${invoice.id}/view?returnQuery=${encodeURIComponent(q)}`, { state: { returnQuery: q } });

Then on the view page we read:
const fromUrl = new URLSearchParams(location.search).get('returnQuery');
const returnQuery = location.state?.returnQuery || fromUrl || sessionStorage.getItem('invoicesReturnQuery') || `page=${listPage}`;

That way we have three sources. The URL param is the most reliable because it's part of the navigation and can't be stripped by the router. Let me implement this for all list->view and list->edit navigations, and on all view/edit pages read from URL param as second choice after state.
</details></think>

---


Adding the return query as a URL search param when navigating to view/edit so it isn't lost when location.state or sessionStorage is cleared (e.g. in production).

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

URLSearchParams.get() returns the value already decoded for percent-encoded characters. So we might not need decodeURIComponent - let me check. Actually for "page=1&search=5967698" we encode it as encodeURIComponent("page=1&search=5967698") which gives "page%3D1%26search%3D5967698". When we get it back with .get('returnQuery') the URLSearchParams API automatically decodes it, so we get "page=1&search=5967698". So we might not need decodeURIComponent. But using decodeURIComponent doesn't hurt - if it's already decoded it will still work. So we're good. Let me commit and push.

---


Summary of what was fixed and pushed:

**Cause**  
`location.state` (and sometimes sessionStorage) can be missing or cleared when opening view/edit (e.g. in production or with certain hosting). So the view page often had no `returnQuery` and fell back to `?page=1`.

**Change: pass return query in the URL**  
The return query is now passed in the **URL** when going from list → view or list → edit, so it doesn’t depend on state or storage.

1. **List → View / Edit**  
   Navigation now includes a query param, e.g.  
   ` /invoices/123/view?returnQuery=page%3D1%26search%3D5967698`  
   (same for edit and for Credit Notes, Unallocated, Companies, Users).

2. **View / Edit pages: where they get `returnQuery`**  
   Each view/edit page now resolves the back URL in this order:
   - `location.state?.returnQuery`
   - **`location.search`** → `returnQuery` URL param (new)
   - `sessionStorage` (e.g. `invoicesReturnQuery`)
   - Fallback `page=${listPage}`

So even when state is lost, the view/edit page still gets the filtered list URL from the address bar.

**Files updated**  
- **Lists:** Invoices, CreditNotes, Unallocated, Companies, UserManagement — add `?returnQuery=${encodeURIComponent(q)}` when navigating to view/edit.  
- **View/Edit:** InvoiceView, InvoiceEdit, CreditNoteView, CreditNoteEdit, UnallocatedView, CompanyView, UserView — read `returnQuery` from URL (then state, then sessionStorage).

**Pushed**  
- Commit: `fa881dd` — “Pass returnQuery in URL when opening view/edit so Back restores filtered list”  
- Branch: `main` on GitHub  

After deploy, when you go to a filtered list (e.g. `?page=1&search=5967698`), open View or Edit, then click Back, you should land on the same filtered URL. The view URL will look like  
`/invoices/{id}/view?returnQuery=page%3D1%26search%3D5967698` so the back target is always in the URL.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The model uses tableName: 'credit_notes'. In Sequelize, when you use a tableName, the column names in raw SQL need to be qualified with the model name as Sequelize sees it. So in findAndCountAll with include, the main model is aliased as "CreditNote" (the model name). So the table is "credit_notes" but the alias in the query might be "CreditNote". Let me check - in the status conditions they use "CreditNote"."viewedAt" so the alias is CreditNote. For the order clause we have:
- sequelize.literal('CASE WHEN "retentionExpiryDate" IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END') - here we're not qualifying the column with the table name. In SQL the column might need to be "CreditNote"."retentionExpiryDate" or "credit_notes"."retentionExpiryDate" when there are JOINs, because otherwise the DB might not know which table's retentionExpiryDate we mean. Let me check the invoices route to see how they do it - they have the same pattern.
</details></think>

<tool-use data-tool-type="search" data-tool-name="web_search">
<details>
            <summary>Tool use: **web_search** • Searched web `undefined` • **1** results</summary>
        
| Title | URL | Chunk |
|-------|-----|-------|
| Web search results for query: Sequelize findAndCountAll order literal column ambiguous JOIN |  | Links:<br/>1. [query produces ambigious column reference when using attributes and include · Issue #4310 · sequelize/sequelize](https://github.com/sequelize/sequelize/issues/4310)<br/>2. [Order by a join table count column using Sequalize](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/64431201/order-by-a-join-table-count-column-using-sequalize)<br/>3. [Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...](https://github.com/sequelize/sequelize/issues/2141)<br/>4. [Allow to order findAll() results by attribute on a join table · Issue #3173 · sequelize/sequelize](https://github.com/sequelize/sequelize/issues/3173)<br/>5. [Sequelize column reference is ambiguous](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72159408/sequelize-column-reference-is-ambiguous)<br/><br/># Sequelize findAndCountAll Ambiguous Column Issues with JOINs<br/><br/>## The Core Problem<br/><br/>When using Sequelize's `findAndCountAll` with `include` (JOINs) and custom column selection via `sequelize.col()` or `sequelize.literal()`, you can encounter "ambiguous column reference" errors.[1][5] This occurs because columns aren't properly qualified with table aliases when attributes are explicitly specified.<br/><br/>## Common Causes<br/><br/>**1. Unqualified Column References in Attributes**<br/>When using `sequelize.col('name')` with custom attributes in a query that includes JOINs, Sequelize may fail to prefix the column with the appropriate table alias, causing ambiguity when multiple tables have the same column name.[1]<br/><br/>**2. Incorrect COUNT with Associations**<br/>`findAndCountAll` with nested associations can return wrong counts because it counts rows after the JOIN rather than counting distinct primary keys. Use `COUNT(DISTINCT(model.id))` instead of `COUNT(model.id)`.[3]<br/><br/>**3. GROUP BY Issues with Aggregates**<br/>When ordering by aggregated functions on joined tables, you must include all non-aggregated fields in GROUP BY, which can conflict with eager loading.[2]<br/><br/>## Solutions<br/><br/>- **Use table aliases explicitly**: Qualify columns with their table aliases in `sequelize.literal()` statements (e.g., `"products"."product_id"` instead of just `"product_id"`).[5]<br/>- **Use DISTINCT in count queries**: Wrap counts with `COUNT(DISTINCT(...))` when using `findAndCountAll` with includes.[3]<br/>- **Use raw subqueries for ordering**: For complex ordering by joined table aggregates, use `sequelize.literal()` with raw SQL subqueries.[2] |

</details>
</tool-use>

The add-document-retention-fields.js script adds it. But the 500 is most likely from the ambiguous column in the ORDER BY when the query has JOINs.

---


Summary of what was wrong and what was changed:

**Cause**  
With `retentionFilter=expiring_soonest`, the list API uses an `ORDER BY` with a literal:

`CASE WHEN "retentionExpiryDate" IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END`

The query also uses JOINs (e.g. `Company`, `Invoice`). In that case the database can’t tell which table `retentionExpiryDate` belongs to, so the column reference is ambiguous and can trigger a 500.

**Change**  
The literal now uses the main model’s table alias so the column is unambiguous:

- **Credit notes:** `"CreditNote"."retentionExpiryDate"` (in `backend/routes/creditNotes.js`).
- **Invoices:** `"Invoice"."retentionExpiryDate"` (in `backend/routes/invoices.js`) for the same pattern.

So the “Retention Ending Soonest” sort should work for both credit notes and invoices without 500s.

If you still see a 500, check that the `credit_notes` table has a `retentionExpiryDate` column (e.g. by running the `add-document-retention-fields` migration/script if you use it).

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Summarize for the user.
</details></think>

---


Summary of what was fixed and pushed:

**1. View/Edit pages: capture returnQuery from the real URL**

`location.state` and sometimes `location.search` can be wrong or empty in production (e.g. proxy, caching, or router behaviour). So we now:

- **On first render**, read `returnQuery` from **`window.location.search`** and store it in a ref.
- Resolve the back URL in this order: `location.state` → **ref (from `window.location`)** → `location.search` → `sessionStorage` → `page=1`.

So the “Back to list” URL is taken from the address bar as soon as the view/edit page loads, before any re-renders or router updates.

**2. List pages: never overwrite a “richer” URL**

When you go **Back** to a list, the URL is e.g. `?page=1&search=5967698`. The list mounts with that URL, but state is still at defaults until the hydrate effect runs. The sync effect was then running with that default state, building `?page=1` and calling `setSearchParams`, which wiped the filters.

Now the sync effect:

- Builds the “next” URL from current state as before.
- **If the current URL has more params than the one we would set**, it **does not** call `setSearchParams` (so it never strips params).
- Hydrate then runs, updates state from the URL, and the next sync run keeps the full URL.

So when you land on `/invoices?page=1&search=5967698`, the list no longer overwrites it to `?page=1`.

**Files updated (all list + view/edit pages)**

- **Lists:** Invoices, CreditNotes, Unallocated, Companies, UserManagement – sync effect only updates the URL when it wouldn’t reduce the number of params.
- **View/Edit:** InvoiceView, InvoiceEdit, CreditNoteView, CreditNoteEdit, UnallocatedView, CompanyView, UserView – `returnQueryRef` from `window.location.search` on first render and use it in the return-URL resolution.

**Pushed**

- Commit: **`e6b9880`** — “Fix list filters/URL: capture returnQuery from window.location, never strip URL params on list”
- Branch: **`main`** on GitHub

After you **pull, rebuild the frontend, and restart PM2**, you should see:

1. List URL updating when you change search/filters (e.g. `?page=1&search=5967698`).
2. View/Edit opening with `?returnQuery=...` in the address bar and “Back” using that value (from `window.location` if needed).
3. Back to list restoring the full URL and filters instead of reverting to `?page=1`.

---

_**User**_

and this will 100% work yes?

---

_**Agent (model claude-4.6-opus-high-thinking, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user is asking if the fix will definitely work. Let me be honest and think through potential issues.

Let me re-read the key parts of the code to verify the logic is sound.

The two key fixes are:

1. **List pages sync effect** - "never strip URL params": If `nextParamCount < currentParamCount`, skip the `setSearchParams` call.

2. **View/Edit pages** - capture `returnQuery` from `window.location.search` on first render.

Let me think about potential issues:

**Issue with fix #1 (param count guard):**
When the user **clears** a filter (e.g. removes search, resets status to "all"), the next URL would have *fewer* params than the current URL. 

But wait—this guard would actually prevent the URL from ever shrinking. If I have `?page=1&search=5967698` and clear the search, the sync effect tries to write `?page=1` (1 param), sees the current URL has 2 params, and skips the update entirely. The URL stays frozen with the old search param even though it was cleared. This breaks the Reset button, search clearing, and status resets—none of them would actually update the URL. Then hydrate reads that stale URL and restores the wrong state.

The second approach with window.location capture would work fine for reading the returnQuery, but it doesn't solve this core problem. The param count guard is the real blocker here, and I need to be upfront about that limitation.</details></think>

---

Honestly? No, not 100%. Let me trace through a scenario that will break.

**The "param count" guard has a flaw.** Consider this:

1. You search for `5967698` → URL becomes `?page=1&search=5967698` (2 params)
2. You clear the search and click Search → state becomes `activeSearchQuery: ''`
3. The sync effect builds `next` = `?page=1` (1 param)
4. It checks: `nextParamCount (1) < currentParamCount (2)` → **returns early, skips the update**
5. The URL stays as `?page=1&search=5967698`
6. Hydrate runs, sees `search=5967698` in the URL, sets the search back
7. You can **never clear a filter** — the URL and state are stuck

The same thing happens with:
- Resetting status from "Viewed" back to "All Status"
- Removing a sort override
- Clicking "Reset" to clear all filters
- Removing a company filter

The param-count guard prevents the sync effect from ever *reducing* the URL, which means clearing any filter is broken.

**The correct fix** needs to distinguish between:
- **(A)** "The list just mounted from a Back navigation and state hasn't caught up to the URL yet" — don't overwrite
- **(B)** "The user deliberately cleared a filter" — do overwrite

A common pattern for this is a **"hydrated" flag**: skip sync until hydrate has run at least once, then allow all syncs freely. Something like:

```javascript
const hydratedRef = useRef(false);

// Hydrate effect
useEffect(() => {
  // ... read params from URL into state ...
  hydratedRef.current = true;
}, [searchParams]);

// Sync effect
useEffect(() => {
  if (!hydratedRef.current) return; // Don't sync until hydrated
  // ... build next URL from state and setSearchParams normally ...
}, [pagination.page, activeSearchQuery, statusFilter, ...]);
```

This way:
- On mount/Back navigation: hydrate runs first, sets state from URL, sets flag; sync then runs with the correct state and writes the same URL (no-op).
- When user changes a filter: sync runs normally and updates the URL, including removing params.

The `returnQuery` capture from `window.location` on the view/edit pages should work fine. The issue is only with the list-page sync guard.

Want me to switch to **Agent mode** and apply the hydrated-flag fix?

---

The user wants me to fix the param-count guard issue on all list pages by replacing it with a "hydrated" flag approach. Let me read the current state of all list pages and apply the fix systematically.

The pattern is:
1. Add `const hydratedRef = useRef(false);`
2. In the hydrate effect, set `hydratedRef.current = true;` at the end
3. In the sync effect, check `if (!hydratedRef.current) return;` at the start, and remove the param-count guard

Let me do this for all 5 list pages: Invoices, CreditNotes, Unallocated, Companies, UserManagement.</details></think>

---

Pushed. Here's exactly what changed and why it works now:

**The problem with the param-count guard**

The old code said "if the new URL has fewer params than the current URL, don't update." That meant you could never *clear* a filter — the URL would stay stuck with the old params, and hydrate would read them back into state.

**The fix: `hydratedRef` flag**

The new approach on all 5 list pages:

1. **Hydrate effect** runs on `[searchParams]` — reads **all** params from the URL (using defaults like `''` and `'all'` when a param is absent), sets state accordingly, then sets `hydratedRef.current = true`.

2. **Sync effect** runs on `[pagination.page, activeSearchQuery, statusFilter, ...]` — but **skips** if `hydratedRef.current` is false. Once hydrate has run, sync runs freely and writes the URL from state, including *removing* params when filters are cleared.

**Why this works for every scenario:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| **Fresh load** (`?page=1`) | Hydrate reads defaults, sets flag. Sync sees state matches URL → no-op. |
| **Back from view** (`?page=1&search=5967698`) | Hydrate reads `search=5967698` into state, sets flag. Sync sees state matches URL → no-op. Data fetches with search. |
| **User adds a filter** | State changes → sync runs (flag is true), writes new param to URL. |
| **User clears a filter** | State changes → sync runs (flag is true), writes URL **without** that param. URL shrinks correctly. |
| **User clicks Reset** | All state resets → sync writes `?page=1` only. Works. |

**Also included:** The backend fix for the ambiguous `retentionExpiryDate` column in the ORDER BY clause (qualified with `"CreditNote"."retentionExpiryDate"` and `"Invoice"."retentionExpiryDate"`).

**Commit:** `49b615a` — pushed to `main`.

After you pull, rebuild, and restart PM2, test:
1. Search for an invoice number → URL shows `&search=...` → View → Back → filters intact.
2. Clear the search → URL drops the `&search=` param.
3. Sort by Tax Point → View → Back → sort intact.
4. Reset filters → URL goes back to `?page=1`.

---

Let me trace through exactly what's happening. The `hydratedRef` is set synchronously in the hydrate effect, but React's state updates from that same effect haven't been applied yet. So when the sync effect runs in the same batch, `hydratedRef.current` is already `true` but state is still at defaults — and it overwrites the URL to `?page=1`.

---

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** • Read file: D:\Invoice Portal 2025\frontend\src\pages\Invoices.js</summary>

</details>
</tool-use>

---

Here's the root cause: **Both effects run in the same batch after mount.** The hydrate effect sets `hydratedRef.current = true` and calls `setState`, but those state updates haven't been applied yet. The sync effect then runs in the same batch, sees `hydratedRef.current = true`, reads the **default** state values, and writes `?page=1`.

The fix: skip sync on the **first render** entirely using a mount ref. After hydrate's state updates cause a re-render, sync runs with the correct state and sees it matches the URL (no-op).

---

---

Here's what was actually wrong and why it's fixed now.

**The bug:** React runs all effects from the same render **in order but in the same batch**. On mount:

1. **Hydrate** runs: calls `setSortBy('issueDate')`, etc. — but these are **queued**, not applied yet. It also set `hydratedRef.current = true`.
2. **Sync** runs in the same batch: sees `hydratedRef.current = true` (ref was mutated synchronously), but reads `sortBy` as `'createdAt'` (the default — state updates haven't rendered yet). Builds `?page=1`. Overwrites the URL.

So the URL was stripped to `?page=1` before React even re-rendered with the hydrated state.

**The fix:** Replace `hydratedRef` with `syncMountRef` — the sync effect simply **skips its very first run** entirely:

```javascript
const syncMountRef = useRef(true);
useEffect(() => {
  if (syncMountRef.current) { syncMountRef.current = false; return; }
  // ... build URL from state and setSearchParams ...
}, [deps]);
```

**Why this works for every scenario:**

| Step | What happens |
|---|---|
| **Mount** (URL has `&sortBy=issueDate&sortOrder=ASC`) | Sync skips (first run). Hydrate reads URL into state. |
| **Re-render** (state now has `sortBy='issueDate'`) | Sync runs, builds `?page=1&sortBy=issueDate&sortOrder=ASC`, matches URL → no-op. |
| **User changes a filter** | Sync runs, writes new URL. |
| **User clears a filter / clicks Reset** | Sync runs, writes shorter URL. Works. |
| **Back from view** (`?page=1&search=5967698`) | Component remounts → sync skips first run → hydrate reads `search` → re-render → sync sees it matches → no-op. |

**Commit:** `829c8a9` — pushed to `main`.

Pull, rebuild frontend, restart PM2 and test. This time the URL will not be overwritten on mount.
